title: Object identity: id() and memory references

overview.what: Every Python object has a unique identity — its location in memory — which you read with id(). The `is` operator compares identity (are these the same object?), while `==` compares value (do they have equal contents?). Two separate lists with the same items are == but not `is`, because they are distinct objects; only a name assigned from another (c = a) shares identity. Think of id() as a house's street address and == as two houses having identical furniture: the same address is literally the same house, identical furniture is not.
overview.why: Confusing `is` with `==` causes real bugs. The safe rule: use `is` only for None, True, and False, and use `==` for comparing values. Checking values with `is` (e.g. x is 1000) can pass or fail unpredictably because only some objects are cached.

why_learning_this: Writing correct None checks (if x is None) in FastAPI handlers and Pydantic validators
Debugging why two 'equal' objects behave differently because they are actually the same object

common_mistakes.title: Using `is` to compare values
common_mistakes.explanation: x is 1000 can be False even when x == 1000, because CPython only caches small integers. Fix: use == for values; reserve is for None, True, and False.

common_mistakes.title: Assuming == means the same object
common_mistakes.explanation: Two dicts can be == yet fully independent, so mutating one will not affect the other. Use `is` when you need to know they are literally the same object.

recall_questions.q: What does the `is` operator compare, versus `==`?
recall_questions.answer: is compares identity (whether they are the same object); == compares value (whether the contents are equal).

recall_questions.q: Two lists have identical contents. Are they ==? Are they `is`?
recall_questions.answer: They are == (equal value) but not is (they are separate objects).

recall_questions.q: When should you use `is` rather than `==`?
recall_questions.answer: Only for None, True, and False.

practice_tasks.title: Fixing the Identity Check
practice_tasks.prompt: The function below incorrectly uses `is` to check if a users input matches a specific integer, and uses `==` to check if a result is `None`. Modify the code to use the correct operators for value comparison and identity checking.

code_walkthrough.focus: a and b are equal (==) but not identical (is); only c, assigned from a, is the same object.

aha_moment.code:
a = 256
b = 256
print(a is b)

big = 1000
print(big is int('1000'))
aha_moment.prediction: Both lines compare equal integers with `is`. What does each print?
aha_moment.why: CPython caches small integers (-5 to 256), so 256 is always that one cached object (is is True). 1000 is outside the cache, and int('1000') builds a fresh object at runtime, so it is a different object (is is False). Use == for values; reserve is for None/True/False.

challenge.title: A correct None check
challenge.prompt: Write get_or_default(value, default) that returns default ONLY when value is None — not when value is 0, '', or an empty list. Use the correct operator, then print three calls to prove 0 and '' are kept.
challenge.solution:
def get_or_default(value, default):
    return default if value is None else value

print(get_or_default(None, 'fallback'))
print(get_or_default(0, 'fallback'))
print(get_or_default('', 'fallback'))

understanding_checks.question: What will be printed when this script is run?
understanding_checks.code:
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = [1, 2, 3]
c = a
print(a == b)
print(a is b)
print(a is c)
understanding_checks.why: `a == b` is True because their contents match. `a is b` is False because `b` is a freshly created list with a different memory address. `a is c` is True because `c` is assigned directly from `a`, so they reference the exact same object in memory.

understanding_checks.question: A developer is writing a validator that ensures a users balance matches exactly 1000. Identify the bug.
understanding_checks.answer: The function uses `is` to check for equality of the value 1000.
understanding_checks.why: Because CPython only caches small integers, using `is` to compare values like 1000 can fail unpredictably depending on how the object was created in memory. To compare values, you must use `==`. `is` should only be used for `None`, `True`, and `False`.
